code:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of triples into a fluent, single (or few) sentence(s).
    Triples that share the same subject are merged into one clause.
    """
    if not triples:
        return ""
    # verb mapping – keep the existing mapping but reuse it
    verb_map = {
        "cityServed": "serves",
        "elevationAboveTheSeaLevel": "has an elevation of",
        "location": "is located at",
        "operatingOrganisation": "is operated by",
        "runwayLength": "has a runway length of",
        "runwayName": "has runway named",
        "country": "is in",
        "isPartOf": "is part of",
        "1stRunwayLengthFeet": "has a first runway length of",
        "1stRunwaySurfaceType": "has a first runway surface of",
        "3rdRunwayLengthFeet": "has a third runway length of",
        "icaoLocationIdentifier": "has ICAO code",
        "locationIdentifier": "has location identifier",
        "elevationAboveTheSeaLevelInFeet": "has elevation of",
        "iataLocationIdentifier": "has IATA code",
        "nativeName": "has native name",
        "leaderParty": "belongs to party",
        "capital": "has capital",
        "language": "speaks",
        "leader": "is led by",
        "owner": "is owned by",
        "1stRunwayLengthMetre": "has a first runway length of",
        "4thRunwaySurfaceType": "has a fourth runway surface of",
        "5thRunwayNumber": "has fifth runway number",
        "largestCity": "has largest city",
        "4thRunwayLengthFeet": "has a fourth runway length of",
        "1stRunwayNumber": "has first runway number",
        "elevationAboveTheSeaLevelInMetres": "has elevation of",
        "administrativeArrondissement": "is in administrative arrondissement",
        "mayor": "has mayor",
        "2ndRunwaySurfaceType": "has second runway surface of",
        "3rdRunwaySurfaceType": "has third runway surface of",
        "runwaySurfaceType": "has runway surface of",
        "officialLanguage": "has official language",
        "city": "is city of",
        "jurisdiction": "has jurisdiction over",
        "demonym": "has demonym",
        "aircraftHelicopter": "operates helicopter",
        "transportAircraft": "operates transport aircraft",
        "currency": "uses currency",
        "headquarter": "has headquarter at",
        "class": "belongs to class",
        "division": "belongs to division",
        "order": "belongs to order",
        "regionServed": "serves region",
        "leaderTitle": "has leader title",
        "hubAirport": "has hub airport",
        "aircraftFighter": "operates fighter aircraft",
        "attackAircraft": "operates attack aircraft",
        "battle": "participated in battle",
        "5thRunwaySurfaceType": "has fifth runway surface of",
        "countySeat": "has county seat",
        "chief": "has chief",
        "foundedBy": "was founded by",
        "postalCode": "has postal code",
        "areaCode": "has area code",
        "foundingYear": "was founded in",
        "ceremonialCounty": "is in ceremonial county",
    }

    # Group triples by their subject to build compact clauses
    from collections import defaultdict
    grouped = defaultdict(list)
    for t in triples:
        verb = verb_map.get(t.predicate, "has")
        # Ensure object is stringified without surrounding quotes
        obj = str(t.object).strip('"')
        grouped[t.subject].append(f"{verb} {obj}")

    # Build sentences for each subject
    clauses = []
    for subj, parts in grouped.items():
        if len(parts) == 1:
            clause = f"{subj} {parts[0]}"
        else:
            # join with commas and an 'and' before the last element
            body = ", ".join(parts[:-1]) + f", and {parts[-1]}"
            clause = f"{subj} {body}"
        clauses.append(clause)

    # Combine different subject clauses with semicolons
    result = "; ".join(clauses) + "."
    return result

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END

You are an expert data engineer specializing in converting data to text. You will be given the current Python program with its BLUE score and an inspiration program and your task will be to evolve the current Python program to improve the score. It is a program that converts a list of triples into natural language text. The given triples will be associated with the topic: Airport. The program must implement a function called 'predict' that accepts a list of triples and generates a coherent, contextually relevant sentence that accurately represents the information contained in the triples. Ensure that the generated text is fluent, grammatically correct, and maintains the meaning of the original data. There can be multiple triples given that make up a complex sentence. Converting all those triples into one sentence will award greater score. An example of a complex sentence:
Triples:
(Antwerp | cityServed | Antwerp International Airport)
(Belgium | country | Antwerp)
(City of Brussels | capital | Belgium)
Example sentence:
"Antwerp International Airport serves the city of Antwerp which is in Belgium, where the capital is Brussels."

The 'predict' function returns that sentence as a string. Below is the list of all possible predicates that can be given as an input. With the given predicates and example triples implement the 'predict' function so it can process all the possible predicates:
